claims.claim: We investigate whether commit-level critic gating—requiring each candidate commit to pass declared invariants and hidden critic tests before acceptance—reduces regression escape rates compared to free-form batch patch generation by AI coding agents.

claims.claim: However, actual Codex-exec experiments yield a neutral result on regression escape rate: both free-form and commit-gated lanes achieve zero escaped regressions in both the 4-candidate and 8-candidate pilots.

claims.claim: Commit gating did catch one incomplete per-commit patch in the harder 8-candidate Codex run (a gift card credit feature missing required support), forcing a repair, but this did not manifest as a final regression delta because the free-form batch also preserved the relevant invariants.

claims.claim: The overhead of commit gating is substantial: wall-time ratios range from 2.53× to 3.34×, and critic-review-time ratios range from 7.92× to 13.38×.

claims.claim: We conclude that commit-level critic gating provides a per-commit quality gate that can catch incomplete patches, but in the tested setting with a strong model, it does not reduce the final regression escape rate enough to justify the observed overhead.

claims.claim: Commit-level critic gating proposes an alternative: require each candidate commit to pass both its declared tests and a set of hidden critic invariants before the commit is accepted into the repository.

claims.claim: The experimental harness (`commit_level_critic_messy_pilot.py`) implements two lanes for the same set of candidate code changes: - **Free-form lane**: The agent receives all maintenance tasks as a single batch, generates patches in one pass, and the result is evaluated against both declared tests and a full set of hidden critic invariants at the end.

claims.claim: Each commit must pass its declared tests plus candidate-scoped critic invariants (base invariants plus features expected to exist by that commit) before acceptance.
